stories.title: Midtown’s 10 Bryant Park Tower Is Back on the Market for More Than $800M
stories.excerpt: An 865,000-square-foot tower along Bryant Park is looking for a new owner. The office tower at 10 Bryant Park, also known as HSBC Tower, is seeking more than $800 million, sources familiar with the plans told Bloomberg. Newmark’s prolific deal-making team, made up of Adam Spies, Adam Doneger and Avery Silverstein, has been tapped to represent the landlord, Tel Aviv-based Property & Building Corporation (PBC). Newmark’s Spies declined to comment on the reported plan. Innovo Property Group previously attempted to purchase 10 Bryant Park for $855 million in 2021, but the deal faced roadblocks in 2022. The 30-story tower, located at 452 Fifth Avenue on the southwest corner of West 40th Street, resides in an especially in-demand Midtown market. Amazon inked a massive 330,000-square-foot deal there in April of last year, two months before gym brand Life Time leased space for a 52,000-square-foot fitness club across four floors. Just this past May, PBC secured a $165 per square foot deal with law firm Baker McKenzie to expand its offices there to 121,833 square feet. The circa-1980s tower was recently renovated, according to reports, and features a new lobby. PBC, a subsidiary of Discount Investment Corporation, is one of Israel’s largest real estate companies. The landlord acquired 10 Bryant Park in 2010 for $330 million. The seller, HSBC Bank USA, continued to lease offices there, but relocated in 2022 to Tishman Speyer’s the Spiral in Hudson Yards. PBC and Discount Investment Corporation could not be immediately reached for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: 10 Bryant Park (HSBC Tower), 865K SF Midtown Manhattan office, seeks $800M+ sale via Newmark.
Amazon (330K SF), Life Time (52K SF), Baker McKenzie (expanding to $165/SF) anchor nearby Midtown corridor.
Prior $855M deal attempt (2021) failed; asset rehiring signals distressed office market stabilization attempt.
stories.summary: Property & Building Corporation is marketing 10 Bryant Park, a 30-story, 865,000-square-foot Midtown Manhattan office tower, for more than $800 million through Newmark. The tower sits in an active submarket where Amazon signed a 330,000-square-foot lease in April 2025, Life Time leased 52,000 square feet for a fitness club, and Baker McKenzie expanded to $165 per square foot. An attempted $855 million sale in 2021 fell through, but current repositioning suggests renewed investor appetite.
stories.impact: Midtown's selective flight to quality office (Class A, high occupancy) is pulling capital away from secondary assets and creating two-tier demand dynamics. High-performing office near major corporate tenants will command premium multiples, but this concentration also signals limited tenant competition for non-prime inventory—developers should target mixed-use or conversion opportunities in Midtown submarkets facing structural office headwinds, not pursue pure office development in direct competition with trophy assets.

stories.title: 2026 Multifamily Leadership Series: Brenda Barrett, Apartment Management Consultants
stories.excerpt: For the fourth year of our annual Leadership Series, Connect CRE has focused on the apartment sector. We invited 18 multifamily heavyweights to provide their unfiltered views of the current market, its challenges and opportunities. The insights of these power players have been collected in a downloadable report, and we’re also presenting them individually, in alphabetical order. The roster begins with Brenda Barrett, CEO of Salt Lake City-based Apartment Management Consultants, which ranked fifth on the National Multifamily Housing Council (NMHC) Top 50 Property Managers list for 2026. Will rising gas prices and inflation affect development? Any rise in inflation and gas prices will inevitably impact the price of building materials and contractor expenses. This, in turn, will drive up the cost of construction in general. Because of this, budgets for any new multifamily development will face this additional challenge and will need to account for and address it. Absorption has increased this year. Is that due to a slowdown in development or other factors? The apartments sector is still adjusting to a major surge in new supply that began to come to market a couple years ago. However, the pace of new construction has since slowed down and allowed for that supply to continue to be absorbed. In addition to the marked slowdown in new construction, there’s another factor helping with absorption. Homeownership remains out of reach for many Americans, keeping a greater number of households in rentals longer and keeping rental demand steady. Will we see an increase or decrease in rent prices this year? Rents in the apartments arena are generally flat. However, it is important to note there is variation in rent growth across markets. Some have recorded negative rent growth in recent years, while others have seen slight rent rate growth over the same period. There are early indications of some stabilization; however the primary drag on rent growth remains that recent surge
bullets: Inflation, gas, contractor costs directly raise multifamily development budgets; absorption up despite new supply slowdown.
Homeownership affordability crisis keeps households renting longer; net demand steady despite muted rent growth nationally.
Rent growth varies by market; generalist developers must localize underwriting to avoid flat-market traps.
stories.summary: Apartment Management Consultants CEO Brenda Barrett notes that rising inflation and gas prices are pushing construction costs and contractor expenses higher, requiring developers to increase budgets. Absorption has improved as new construction slowed, and persistently high homeownership costs are keeping renters in apartments longer. However, rents nationally remain flat with significant market-by-market variation, requiring tailored underwriting by submarket rather than broad assumptions.
stories.impact: Tightening development margins (rising hard costs) coupled with flat rents in many markets narrows feasibility windows; developers must identify submarkets with structural demographic tailwinds (homeownership gap, job growth) to achieve rent-growth offsets. Slower starts are reducing near-term supply pressure, but conversion projects and value-add repositioning in high-demand pockets are more attractive than greenfield development in markets with negative rent growth.

stories.title: Manhattan Investment Sales Tally Reaches $10B in H1 2026
stories.excerpt: Manhattan remained the primary driver of New York City’s investment sales market during the first half of 2026 with total dollar volume increasing 50% year-over year to $9.87 billion across 238 transactions, according to Ariel Property Advisors’ Manhattan 2026 Mid- Year Commercial Real Estate Trends report. The tally marked the borough’s strongest first half since 2022. “Manhattan office properties led all asset classes in H1 2026, driven by a 31% surge in dollar volume to $3.53 billion,” said Michael A. Tortorici, Ariel founding partner. “Acquisitions of prime Class A properties–which are seeing tighter vacancy and promising long-term upside– represent a significant vote of confidence in the city’s economic trajectory.” A highlight of H1 2026 was SL Green Realty Corp.’s $730-million purchase of Park Avenue Tower at 65 E. 55th St. from Blackstone’s Perform Properties, which closed in January. The 621,000-square-foot office asset maintained 96.5% occupancy at closing, The post Manhattan Investment Sales Tally Reaches $10B in H1 2026 appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Manhattan investment sales H1 2026: $9.87B (+50% YoY), 238 transactions; strongest first half since 2022.
Office led: $3.53B (+31% YoY); Class A, tight-vacancy properties drawing confidence in economic trajectory.
SL Green bought Park Avenue Tower (621K SF, 96.5% occupied) from Blackstone for $730M in January.
stories.summary: Manhattan commercial investment sales reached $9.87 billion in the first half of 2026, up 50 percent year-over-year and the strongest first half since 2022. Office assets led all categories with $3.53 billion in volume, driven by acquisitions of prime Class A properties with tight vacancy and strong occupancy fundamentals. SL Green's $730 million purchase of Park Avenue Tower (96.5% leased, 621K SF) exemplified the market's focus on quality assets with long-term upside.
stories.impact: Manhattan office investment momentum is rotating toward trophy assets and away from secondary stock, signaling that capital deployed in Class A office is redirecting from multifamily. This capital reallocation pressures multifamily development financing in Manhattan and suggests residential developers should focus on mixed-use conversions or value-add repositioning rather than competing for land in office-heavy submarkets; conversely, secondary Manhattan neighborhoods with weak office but strong residential demand may offer acquisition opportunities as office holders capitulate.

stories.title: Chicago/Midwest People & Company News, week of July 24, 2026
stories.excerpt: CBRE announced that Laurel Keller has joined the firm as a first vice president in its Columbus office for CBRE Hotels. In this role, Keller works alongside Eric Belfrage and Michael Shirey, specializing in hotel investment sales nationwide and advising clients on acquisitions and dispositions while optimizing real estate value and investment performance. Based in the Midwest, she serves hospitality investors, owners and operators across the country. CBRE also announced that Ajay Kalra has been appointed managing director and market leader for its Omaha, Nebraska, business, in addition to his role overseeing the firm’s Iowa business. In this expanded role, Kalra will be responsible for all day-to-day operations for the company’s advisory services in Omaha and Iowa, including leasing, sales, valuations, and debt and structured finance across all property types. The appointment follows the transition of Bennett Ginsberg, formerly Omaha market leader, to a full-time producer role with CBRE. Ambrose announced key promotions and new hires across its growing team. The announcement follows the recent hiring of Courtney Schneider as vice president and market officer for the Mountain States region. Clay Smith has been promoted to director of development, and Kendrick Crisp has been promoted to accounting manager. Ambrose also welcomed new hires, including Anne Stewart, who has joined Ambrose as chief of staff, supporting founder & CEO Aasif Bade in advancing key strategic priorities across the organization. Leslie Hobbs has joined as vice president of accounting, while Sean Steidler has joined as vice president of financial planning and analysis. Additionally, Natalie Peters has joined as director of investor relations, Jake Okerson has joined as an accountant, and Melodee Allen has joined as an accounting operations specialist. Cathy Roth has also joined Ambrose as office and operations coordinator, where she will oversee day-to-day office operations. JLL announced the
bullets: CBRE expanded leadership in Columbus (hotels), Omaha/Iowa (all property types); internal restructuring/promotions.
Ambrose appointed Mountain States market officer; added CFO-level staff (Chief of Staff, VP Finance/FPA, Director IR).
Minor staffing moves; limited direct demand/supply/capital signals for multifamily development.
stories.summary: CBRE promoted leadership in Columbus and Omaha/Iowa markets and Ambrose expanded its management team with hires in finance, planning, and investor relations. These moves indicate brokerage and services-firm scaling but carry limited operational signals about multifamily development fundamentals.
stories.impact: No material impact on multifamily development—story is internal management reshuffling at advisory and brokerage firms. Recommend skip for morning briefing.

stories.title: Greystone Lends $47M for Long Island Section 8 Housing Complex
stories.excerpt: Metropolitan Realty Group has sealed $46.8 million of agency debt for the acquisition and refinancing of a Long Island affordable housing complex, Commercial Observer has learned. Greystone provided the nonrecourse, fixed-rate Fannie Mae-backed loan on the developer’s Pine Town Houses Section 8 garden-style apartment complex in Long Beach, N.Y. The nonrecourse deal, which was originated by Eric Rosenstock, senior managing director at Greystone, and Jeff Englund, executive vice president, carries a 10-year term with five years of interest-only payments followed by a 35-year amortization schedule. “Greystone understands the complexities of affordable housing financing in every market cycle,” Rosenstock said in a statement. Located at 151 East Pine Street, the 1975-built Pine Town Houses property is composed of eight two-story buildings with 130 units ranging from one to four bedrooms. The property, which is supported by long-term Section 8 Housing Assistance Payments, will undergo a “moderate rehabilitation” project as part of the refi, according to Greystone. Greystone also provided Fannie Mae-backed loans of $25.7 million and $19.4 million on Metropolitan Realty Group’s Hudson View II and Hudson View III properties, respectively, in northern Manhattan’s Hamilton Heights neighborhood. Metropolitan Realty Group acquired the portfolio of 129 Section 8 units for $45 million from Aminim Group, New York Business Journal previously reported. Planned upgrades across all three properties include interior apartment renovations, building system improvements and enhancements to common areas, according to Greystone. “The preservation of affordable housing remains one of the most important priorities in our industry,” Scott Jaffee, president of Metropolitan Realty Group, said in a statement. “Every transaction represents an opportunity to help owners secure long-term financing that strengthens their properties, preserves affordability and supports the comm
bullets: Greystone closed $46.8M Fannie Mae nonrecourse loan for Metropolitan Realty Group, Pine Town Houses Long Beach, NY.
130-unit Section 8 garden-style complex (1975-built); 10-year fixed-rate, 5-yr IO / 35-yr amort; moderate rehab planned.
Agency debt availability supports affordable housing acquisitions; stable Section 8 income underpins long-term hold fundamentals.
stories.summary: Greystone provided a $46.8 million Fannie Mae-backed nonrecourse loan for Metropolitan Realty Group to acquire and refinance Pine Town Houses, a 130-unit Section 8 garden-style apartment complex in Long Beach, New York. The 10-year fixed-rate loan carries five years of interest-only payments followed by a 35-year amortization, with modest rehabilitation planned.
stories.impact: Agency debt availability for stabilized, long-term Section 8-backed assets remains strong, supporting acquisitions and value-add plays in affordable housing. Developers with Section 8 portfolios or targeting acquisition of stabilized affordable properties benefit from predictable financing; conversely, new construction affordable housing (requires LIHTC layering) faces tighter capital stack discipline and higher development risk—focus capital on existing-asset plays or ground-up development in high-subsidy markets (NY, CA, MA).

stories.title: Skanska Shares Rendering of Bellevue Mass-Timber Office Tower
stories.excerpt: Skanska announced continued progress on Bevel, its planned 12-story mass-timber office tower in downtown Bellevue. The project remains in the architectural design and entitlement phase with the City of Bellevue, as Skanska, Mithun + Waugh Thistleton, and other project partners continue to advance plans for the sustainable office development. Located at the corner of 106th Ave. NE and NE 9th Pl., Bevel is planned as an approximately 200,000-square-foot workplace environment. The project will be adjacent to The Eight — Skanska’s recently completed office tower in Bellevue, which is currently 90% leased. When completed, Bevel is expected to be among the tallest mass-timber office towers in the U.S. and one of the most significant commercial mass-timber office developments in the commercial real estate industry. “Building on the success of The Eight and the strong Eastside market, Bevel represents an incredible opportunity for a future partner to establish a new world-class office or HQ on all levels,” said Charlie Foushée, Executive Vice President and Regional Manager for Skanska USA Commercial Development’s Seattle market. The post Skanska Shares Rendering of Bellevue Mass-Timber Office Tower appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Skanska's Bevel: 12-story, 200K SF mass-timber office tower in downtown Bellevue; entitlement phase underway.
Adjacent to The Eight (90% leased); targets tallest/most significant mass-timber office tower in U.S.
Product-type innovation (timber construction) for office; limited demand signal for multifamily.
stories.summary: Skanska is advancing permitting on Bevel, a 12-story, 200,000-square-foot mass-timber office tower in downtown Bellevue scheduled for construction. The project sits next to The Eight, Skanska's recently completed 90%-leased office tower, and is positioned as one of the U.S.'s tallest mass-timber commercial office developments.
stories.impact: Mass-timber office development is niche product innovation; does not signal demand for multifamily. Indirectly, Bellevue's office pipeline (The Eight strong leasing + Bevel entitlements) suggests tight office supply and strong Eastside Seattle market fundamentals, which may support multifamily demand if office employment expands—but this is secondary. Developers in Seattle metro should monitor Bellevue office absorption and tenant profiles (tech, HQ) to gauge upstream wage growth and household formation demand.

stories.title: Report: Domestic Migration Continues Shaping Local Economies
stories.excerpt: Although Florida and Texas are touted as the strongest magnets for in-migration, South Carolina and Delaware actually set the pace last year, Placer.ai says in a new white paper. With the exodus from coastal markets seen during the pandemic having cooled, no state recorded net inflows or outflows exceeding 0.7% of its starting population in 2025. “[[R]return-to-office mandates, higher mortgage rates, and a shrinking affordability gap between coastal cities and many COVID-era hotspots have dampened the incentive to move,” according to Placer.ai’s Migration After the Boom: Where Americans Are Moving in 2026. “But even in a slower market, domestic migration remains one of the most powerful forces shaping local economies, housing markets, and consumer demand.” The new report leverages AI-powered location analytics to examine the relocation patterns reshaping the U.S. It analyzes where Americans are moving, the demographic and economic forces driving those decisions, and how retailers, investors, developers, and policymakers can respond to the opportunities and challenges created by these shifts. The post Report: Domestic Migration Continues Shaping Local Economies appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: South Carolina, Delaware led 2025 domestic migration; Florida/Texas no longer pandemic-era standouts.
Return-to-office mandates, higher mortgage rates, shrinking affordability gap dampened migration incentive; no state exceeded 0.7% net inflow/outflow.
Migration remains powerful force; slower pace requires market-specific demand analysis.
stories.summary: A Placer.ai report reveals that South Carolina and Delaware topped domestic migration in 2025, with Florida and Texas no longer dominating inflows as in pandemic years. Return-to-office mandates, higher mortgage rates, and narrowing affordability gaps between coastal and sunbelt markets have slowed migration velocity; no state experienced net population shifts exceeding 0.7% of starting population.
stories.impact: The migration mega-wave is cooling—developers cannot rely on broad sunbelt tailwinds or pandemic-era assumptions. South Carolina and Delaware outperformance (specific submarkets: Charleston SC, Wilmington DE) warrant targeted acquisition/development focus; conversely, Florida and Texas markets previously priced on high-migration premiums face rent and occupancy headwinds if local job growth does not replace relocated-worker demand. Developers must anchor underwriting to local employment growth (corporate HQs, advanced manufacturing, tech, healthcare) rather than demographic flow assumptions; cities with strong return-to-office mandates (NYC, SF, Chicago) may see repricing of suburban multifamily as remote-work softens and demand returns to core.

stories.title: Blackstone Reports Record AUM, Strong Earnings Powered by Data Centers, AI
stories.excerpt: Blackstone’s growth continued unabated in the first half of 2026, as the firm leveraged its enormous data center and AI platform to report strong shareholder returns. In the second quarter, the firm delivered more than 20 percent year-over-year growth across total revenues, fee revenues, fee-related earnings, net realization and distributable earnings, according to Michael Chae, vice chairman and chief financial officer. “The firm’s continued evolution and the expanding scope of our activity have fundamentally transformed our earnings power, both in terms of the magnitude and breadth and sources of earnings,” said Chae. The private equity powerhouse reported that its distributable earnings increased 26 percent year-over-year to $2 billion, with fee-related earnings growing 22 percent to $1.7 billion, or $1.29 per share, and net realizations rising by 27 percent, all despite geopolitical headwinds. Capital inflows into Blackstone reached $70 billion in the second quarter, and now stand at $260 billion in the last 12 months alone, increasing the firm’s total assets under management (AUM) 11 percent year-over-year to a firm-record $1.35 trillion. “Blackstone reported outstanding second-quarter results,” said Blackstone CEO Stephen Schwarzman. “The most significant driver of these strong results continues to be the large-scale investments we’ve made in artificial intelligence-related areas, including data centers, energy and power, and the frontier AI companies themselves.” Schwarzman noted that Blackstone has become one of the biggest private capital providers in the entire AI ecosystem, largely by building what he called “the largest data center development business in the world.” That growth has come through the firm’s 2021 acquisition of QTS Data Centers, and its direct investments into Anthropic, OpenAI, Google and SpaceX. Schwarzman also pointed out that in the second quarter Blackstone launched Blackstone Digital Infrastructure Trust (known by its stock symbol BX
bullets: Blackstone Q2 2026: 20%+ YoY growth across revenues, fee earnings, net realizations; $70B capital inflows.
Data center and AI platform drove earnings acceleration; distributable earnings +26% YoY to $2B.
Capital mobility toward data center/AI; marginal multifamily competitive disadvantage for capital.
stories.summary: Blackstone reported over 20 percent year-over-year growth in Q2 2026 driven by its data center and AI platforms, with distributable earnings rising 26 percent to $2 billion and capital inflows reaching $70 billion. The firm's earnings power has shifted toward these high-growth, capital-intensive infrastructure sectors despite geopolitical headwinds.
stories.impact: Blackstone's capital flow acceleration into data centers and AI signals a structural shift of dry powder away from core multifamily; larger developers with GPs' ear (Blackstone, Brookfield, etc.) are repricing multifamily assets and recalibrating equity deployment. For mid-market and regional multifamily developers, this means tighter institutional capital access, higher hurdle rates on competing deals, and potential secondary-market dislocation as Tier-1 capital exits. Developers should expect tighter spreads, slower capital deployment timelines, and pressure to demonstrate alternative (e.g., affordable, senior housing, climate resilience) thesis to attract institutional dry powder.

stories.title: Mandarin Oriental Brickell Key, Live Local high-rise among $420M-plus South Florida construction permits for week ending July 22, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest permit issued was a $240 million new construction permit for 1 Island Drive in Miami, where Swire Properties is developing The Residences at Mandarin Oriental, Miami and Mandarin Oriental Hotel on Brickell Key. The former Mandarin Oriental Hotel towers on the site were demolished via controlled implosion in April. The redevelopment will include condos and a hotel, totaling about 1.35 million square feet. Job Filing Number: BD23028760001B001 📊 New Building: The second-largest permit was $95 million for 114 Northeast 22nd Street in Miami, where New Sense LLC, an affiliate of Argentina-based HA Emprendimientos, is developing […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: South Florida permits week ending July 22, 2026: $420M+ total; Mandarin Oriental redevelopment $240M (Miami).
Mandarin Oriental: condos + hotel, 1.35M SF on Brickell Key; Sense 22 (Live Local mixed-use, 320+ units, $95M permit, $111M construction loan).
High permit velocity, major mixed-use delivers signal strong South Florida multifamily demand and construction financing availability.
stories.summary: South Florida issued over $420 million in construction permits in the week ending July 22, 2026, led by a $240 million permit for Swire Properties' Mandarin Oriental redevelopment on Brickell Key (condos + hotel, 1.35M SF) and a $95 million permit for Sense 22, a 36-story, 503,000-square-foot Live Local mixed-use project with over 320 units. Sense 22 secured a $111 million construction loan from S3 Capital.
stories.impact: Brickell Key and mixed-use multifamily activity shows robust South Florida multifamily demand and construction financing appetite (large construction loans closing for 320+ unit projects). Developers should prioritize Miami/Brickell multifamily acquisition and entitlement; high permit flow and large debt commitments indicate capital abundance and tenant demand for new-construction units. Mixed-use (retail-residential) is preferred product in South Florida coastal markets; pure multifamily faces higher entitlement risk. Monitor absorptions post-delivery (H2 2027 completion timeframes) to confirm rent growth trajectory amid supply acceleration.

stories.title: Durst Secures Three Leases at 1133 Avenue of the Americas
stories.excerpt: The Durst Organization has announced three leases totaling 11,223 square feet for prebuilt office suites at the company’s 1133 Avenue of the Americas, Commercial Observer has learned. In the largest of these, software company Quantexa signed a new lease for 5,263 square feet at the building. The U.K.-based firm specializes in artificial intelligence-based software applications for analysis and decision-making, according to the company website. In addition, theater company Olympus Theatricals renewed its lease for 3,345 square feet, and financial services firm Global Securitization Services renewed for 2,615 square feet. The lengths of the three leases were unclear. The Durst Organization was represented in-house by Tom Bow, Rocco Romeo and Nora Caliban. Quantexa was represented by Aidan Campbell of Colliers. “These three leases reflect what tenants are looking for right now: quality space in a best-in-class building,” said Jody Durst, president of the Durst Organization. “We are grateful that Olympus Theatricals and Global Securitization Services have recommitted to 1133 Avenue of the Americas, and welcome Quantexa to the building.” 1133 Avenue of the Americas is a 1.1 million-square-foot, 45-story Class A tower a block north of Bryant Park. Global banking and financial services firm ING Group renewed its 132,400-square-foot space at the building in December 2025, adding 21,515 square feet more for a total of 153,915 square feet throughout the seventh through 11th floors, as reported in CO. Law firm Massumi + Consoli also moved into 22,915 square feet there with a seven-year lease in August 2025. The move was an expansion for the firm, which Durst said now had five times more space than it had in its previous home at Penn 1. The month before, another law firm, Steptoe, signed a 15-year lease at 1133 Avenue of the Americas for 57,988 square feet on the building’s 43rd, 44th and 45th floors. The firm had previously been headquartered at 1114 Sixth Avenue. As of July 2
bullets: Durst Organization: three office leases at 1133 Avenue of the Americas (Manhattan), 11.2K SF total.
Quantexa (AI software, 5.3K SF new), Olympus Theatricals (3.3K SF renewal), Global Securitization (2.6K SF renewal).
Tertiary office leasing reflects niche demand in trophy buildings; marginal signal for multifamily.
stories.summary: The Durst Organization executed three office leases totaling 11,223 square feet at 1133 Avenue of the Americas in Manhattan, including a new lease to AI-software firm Quantexa (5,263 SF) and renewals by theater company Olympus Theatricals (3,345 SF) and financial services firm Global Securitization Services (2,615 SF). The leases were in prebuilt office suites marketed as quality space in a best-in-class building.
stories.impact: Boutique office leasing in trophy Manhattan buildings signals selective tenant interest in premium real estate but does not indicate broad office demand recovery. Durst's success reflects building-specific quality/location rather than office market strength. Limited signal for multifamily developers except indirect: concentration of office activity in Midtown trophy assets means secondary and outer-borough office is weakening, creating conversion opportunities (office-to-residential) in Durst-class buildings and supporting mixed-use repositioning in Manhattan suburbs. Developers should identify distressed secondary office assets in Brooklyn, Long Island City, Astoria for conversion.

stories.title: UrbanStreet targets former Motorola office for residential conversion
stories.excerpt: UrbanStreet Group is planning a residential conversion of an atrium office building that used to house Motorola, as part of Schaumburg’s the District at Veridian development. During an informal presentation with village officials, UrbanStreet managing partner Bob Burk said that converting the six-story, 360,200-square-foot building would be a “poster child” for redevelopment creativity, according to the Chicago Daily Herald. The project also includes the construction of a sister building that would reach as high as four stories, giving space for the complex to have around 300 units. 18 row houses will connect the complex to D.R. Horton’s Northgate at Veridian […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: UrbanStreet converting 6-story, 360K SF former Motorola office (Schaumburg) to ~300-unit apartment complex.
Sister 4-story building; District at Veridian $1B megadevelopment; first phase 9-story building nearing completion by early 2027.
Office-to-residential conversion, $94M construction loan (Bank OZK, 2025), strong Chicago suburban multifamily pipeline.
stories.summary: UrbanStreet Group is converting a six-story, 360,200-square-foot former Motorola office building in Schaumburg into approximately 300 apartments as part of the District at Veridian megadevelopment. The complex will be anchored by a four-story sister building and connected to D.R. Horton's Northgate residential project. The first phase nine-story apartment building is expected to finish construction by early 2027; UrbanStreet funded the $175 million first phase with a $94 million construction loan from Bank OZK in 2025.
stories.impact: Office-to-residential conversion in Chicago suburbs signals both supply opportunity and demand fundamentals: successful conversion indicates adjacent residential demand strong enough to absorb 300+ units while competing with D.R. Horton. Bank OZK's $94M commitment and project scale ($1B total) show regional construction financing appetite. Developers should target similar obsolete office campuses in Chicago suburbs (Aurora, Naperville, Rosemont) for conversion; construction cost offsets (shell, parking, loading) favor suburban office conversion over greenfield. Monitor District at Veridian absorption (first phase 2027 completion) as barometer for suburban Chicago multifamily rent/lease-up—if strong, secondary suburban corridors warrant aggressive land acquisition.

stories.title: New York’s Temporary Data Center Moratorium Could Mean Permanent Losses
stories.excerpt: On July 14, New York Gov. Kathy Hochul signed the nation’s first statewide moratorium on the building of new hyperscale data centers, pausing all state environmental permits for up to one year. The purpose, according to the governor’s announcement, is to take the time to establish “a nation-leading regulatory framework that protects ratepayers, the environment, the energy grid and communities across the state.” But, at a time when data center construction has become something of a national gold rush, with states competing for the jobs and economic activity new data center activity brings, many are asking if the moratorium will cost New York dearly in terms of economic growth. According to engineering and supply chain intelligence platform Accuris, the top five hyperscalers — Amazon, Microsoft, Google, Meta and Oracle — are “projected to spend over $600 billion on infrastructure in 2026, a 36 percent increase from 2025.” And, for another illustration of just how quickly the industry is evolving, in mid-2025, Goldman Sachs projected that hyperscaler capital expenditure from 2025 through 2027 would reach $1.15 trillion, a massive increase compared to the $477 billion spent from 2022 through 2024. But the investment bank drastically revised its own estimates barely a year later, and now projects that hyperscaler capex in 2027 alone could reach $1.1 trillion. It makes sense, then, that some states are fiercely competing for this spending and the jobs it could bring, and that when a state like New York hits pause, states with more lax regulatory frameworks gain ground on bringing those dollars and jobs home. Texas and Virginia lead the nation in both current data center capacity and the willingness to indulge in as much additional data center construction as possible. According to market intelligence company Cleanview, which tracks large hyperscale data centers with identifiable power capacity, Virginia currently has 371 operating data centers with a combined capacity of
bullets: NY Gov. Hochul signed first statewide hyperscale data center moratorium (July 14, 2026); pauses environmental permits up to 1 year.
Hyperscalers (Amazon, Microsoft, Google, Meta, Oracle) projected to spend $600B+ infrastructure in 2026 (+36% YoY); $1.15T spend 2025-2027.
Regulatory block diverts massive capital flow from NY; indirect benefit to competing states; marginal multifamily impact.
stories.summary: New York Governor Kathy Hochul signed the nation's first statewide moratorium on hyperscale data center construction, pausing all state environmental permits for up to one year to establish a regulatory framework. The moratorium arrives as the top five hyperscalers (Amazon, Microsoft, Google, Meta, Oracle) are projected to spend over $600 billion on infrastructure in 2026, a 36 percent increase from 2025, with $1.15 trillion expected through 2027.
stories.impact: The moratorium diverts massive hyperscaler capex away from New York, indirectly redirecting investment to competing states (Virginia, Texas, Ohio). For multifamily developers, the indirect effect is loss of incremental job-driven demand in NY that would have supported rent growth in tech-adjacent markets (Brooklyn, Long Island City). Expect multifamily rent-growth deceleration in NYC due to foregone tech employment influx. Conversely, developers in Texas, Virginia, and Ohio—states competing for data center investment—should anticipate secondary job-creation demand (construction, logistics, real estate services) supporting multifamily demand in regional growth corridors adjacent to hyperscaler campuses. No immediate multifamily supply impact but long-term demand headwind for NYC metro.

stories.title: As Deerfield loosens waterfront development rules, West Palm tightens them
stories.excerpt: Two South Florida coastal cities are moving in opposite directions on waterfront development, as localities grapple with regulating construction on the region’s most valuable real estate.  Deerfield Beach is considering looser guidelines for building on the waterfront, while West Palm Beach has temporarily reined in development.  Deerfield Beach commissioners voted 3-2 Tuesday for charter amendments that would loosen decadeslong restrictions, including eliminating floor area ratio maximums for commercial and multifamily properties between the Intracoastal Waterway and the Atlantic Ocean, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The change also would lift limits on mixed-use developments and projects with accessory use that […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Deerfield Beach loosens waterfront zoning: eliminates FAR maximums for multifamily/commercial (Intracoastal to Atlantic); vote Aug. 4, referendum Nov. 3.
West Palm Beach tightens waterfront rules; opposite trajectory within South Florida coastal markets.
Zoning divergence creates arbitrage opportunity for developers; Deerfield Beach waterfront multifamily feasibility improves.
stories.summary: Deerfield Beach commissioners voted 3-2 to loosen decades-old waterfront development restrictions, including eliminating floor area ratio maximums for commercial and multifamily properties between the Intracoastal Waterway and the Atlantic Ocean. A second vote is scheduled for August 4, with final voter approval set for a November 3 referendum. In contrast, West Palm Beach has temporarily tightened waterfront development rules, creating divergent coastal development policies within South Florida.
stories.impact: Deerfield Beach's FAR removal for waterfront multifamily dramatically increases density and development value on constrained coastal land, making waterfront multifamily projects feasible where previously capped by density limits. Developers should acquire or option waterfront parcels in Deerfield Beach before referendum passes (Nov. 3) and entitlements shift; post-approval, land values and construction pipelines will accelerate. West Palm Beach's tightening, by contrast, narrows multifamily development appetite in that submarket—developers should reallocate coastal resources to Deerfield Beach, Pompano, Fort Lauderdale. Long Island coastal multifamily may also benefit from Deerfield's signal of looser zoning, if South Florida market acceptance reduces regulatory risk appetite elsewhere.

stories.title: Alonzo Mourning, HTG net $55M financing package for affordable housing
stories.excerpt: NBA hall-of-famer Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group scored a $55.4 million financing package for a Naranja affordable housing complex. The deal comes as the duo has racked up financing in recent years for below-market rate rentals in Miami-Dade County.  Mourning, through his AM Affordable Housing, and Coconut Grove-based Housing Trust Group plan the 115-unit Artisan Pointe project at 26115 South Dixie Highway in an unincorporated area of south Miami-Dade County. The four-story complex is under construction with completion expected in January 2028, according to the developers’ news release.  The financing package includes $31.5 million in low-income housing tax credit […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Alonzo Mourning, Housing Trust Group: $55.4M financing for Artisan Pointe, 115-unit South Miami-Dade affordable housing.
$31.5M LIHTC equity, $16.9M permanent loan (Berkadia), $7M Naranja CRA (tax-increment + loan); completion January 2028.
Rents: 30% AMI 1BR = $766; 70% AMI 2BR = $2,145; below-market vs. Naranja avg $1,350–$2,250; LIHTC + CRA stack model replicable.
stories.summary: NBA Hall of Famer Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group financed a 115-unit affordable housing project (Artisan Pointe) in Naranja, South Miami-Dade, with a $55.4 million package comprising $31.5 million in LIHTC equity, a $16.9 million permanent mortgage arranged by Berkadia, and $7 million from the Naranja Lakes Community Redevelopment Agency (half tax-increment funding, half loan). The four-story project targets households at 30–70 percent AMI and is scheduled for completion in January 2028.
stories.impact: The LIHTC + CRA tax-increment financing stack demonstrates viable capital structure for South Florida affordable multifamily in secondary submarkets (Naranja, unincorporated Miami-Dade). Developers should target similar CRA zones with tax-increment revenue and LIHTC availability; Naranja's model is replicable in Miami-Dade's redevelopment corridors and adjacent unincorporated areas. Supply tailwind: Artisan Pointe is phase one of a larger master-plan; expect continued affordable housing production in Naranja/South Miami-Dade. Demand confirmed: 30% AMI rents ($766/1BR) below-market, indicating strong affordable tenant demand. Developers entering South Florida affordable housing should replicate Mourning/HTG capital stack (LIHTC + permanent loan + CRA equity) and target similar CRA-eligible sites.

stories.title: Endeavor boosts Austin office pipeline with 5th and Walsh project
stories.excerpt: Endeavor’s 5th and Walsh project in Downtown Austin, which will also house its new headquarters, is finally getting off the ground.  Plans for the new five-story, 198,000-square-foot office mid-rise and retail building were submitted to the city in March 2022, more than four years ago. The development will feature 21,000 square feet of restaurant and retail space on the ground floor and a below-grade parking garage, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The project is expected to be completed in the later months of 2028, and Endeavor will promptly move into the building from its old headquarters at 500 West […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor 5th and Walsh office project, Downtown Austin: 198K SF, 5-story, delayed 4+ years; 50%+ preleased before groundbreak.
Endeavor relocation from 500 W. 5th to 1200 W. 5th; retail/restaurant ground floor, parking; completion late 2028.
Amazon advanced robotics facility in adjacent Dog's Head area (45-yr development agreement); Austin office pipeline stalls; Endeavor HQ anchor supports office.
stories.summary: Endeavor's 5th and Walsh office project in Downtown Austin, a 198,000-square-foot, five-story mid-rise with 21,000 square feet of retail/restaurant space, is moving forward after four years of entitlements. The project is already 50 percent preleased (85,000 SF tenant commitments plus Endeavor's 25,000 SF HQ), though groundbreaking has yet to occur. Completion is targeted for late 2028. Amazon is separately pursuing an advanced robotics manufacturing facility in Endeavor-owned Dog's Head area under a 45-year development agreement.
stories.impact: Endeavor's large HQ anchor + 50% preleasing (on unstarted project) signals strong Downtown Austin office demand, contradicting broader office pipeline slowdown. However, this is company-specific (Endeavor HQ relocation) rather than market-wide recovery signal. Amazon's robotics facility is major employment driver—expect multifamily demand tailwind in Dog's Head submarket and wider Austin suburbs as job creation materializes (facility construction 2027–2029+). Developers should stage residential development near robotics campus and Downtown Austin for post-2028 delivery aligned with Amazon employment ramp. Austin multifamily remains strong (tech, corporate relocation backdrop) but office-driven supply concentration risk—diversify with amenity-rich, younger-demo product to capture Amazon workforce influx.

stories.title: Starwood files data center plan as Virginia residents revolt
stories.excerpt: Barry Sternlicht’s Northern Virginia data center drive is forging ahead, even as residents plan to revolt against Starwood Capital Group’s dreams. Starwood filed a permit application for its two-building project at the 34-acre Plaza 500 on the Alexandria town line in Virginia, the Washington Business Journal reported. The first building, Plaza 500 South, is planned for more than 226,000 square feet, according to the pending application. HKS Inc. is the architect for the project. The first phase of construction is expected to carry a $5 million price tag, according to Fairfax County records. In addition to the two data center […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Starwood Capital (Barry Sternlicht) filed data center permit for Plaza 500 (34 acres, Alexandria VA town line); 226K+ SF first phase.
Proposed substation within 100 ft of homes; resident opposition; Starwood owns site (purchased 4 yrs ago for $165M), purchased adjacent Chantilly site ($166.8M).
Regulatory/zoning friction; Northern Virginia data center pipeline active; marginal multifamily competitive pressure for land/capital.
stories.summary: Starwood Capital Group filed a permit application for a two-building hyperscale data center project at Plaza 500 in Alexandria, Virginia, with the first phase (226,000+ SF) budgeted at $5 million. The project includes a substation within 100 feet of residential homes, sparking community opposition; Starwood purchased the site four years ago for $165 million and also acquired an adjacent Chantilly property for $166.8 million, which residents proposed as an alternative site. Planning commission review is scheduled for September.
stories.impact: Starwood's Northern Virginia data center push is part of broader hyperscaler capex competition with NY-focused rivals (Blackstone, others). The project faces regulatory and community friction, which may delay or derisk Starwood's timeline—developers should monitor outcome (September planning vote) as barometer for Virginia's capacity to absorb hyperscaler demand. Indirectly, massive data center land purchases ($165M + $166.8M) in Northern Virginia signal capital diversion from residential development; tight land availability and rising land costs for multifamily in Alexandria/Fairfax County. Developers should focus on transit-oriented, infill multifamily in Northern Virginia suburbs and avoid competing for large greenfield sites where hyperscalers are dominant bidders.
